subject: Chemistry

learning_objectives.statement: Calculate the values of pH and pOH, based on Kw and the concentration of all species present in a neutral solution of water.

The concentrations of hydronium ion and hydroxide ion are often reported as pH and pOH, respectively. EQN: pH = -log[H3O+] EQN: pOH = -log[OH-] The terms "hydrogen ion" and "hydronium ion" and the symbols H+(aq) and H3O+(aq) are often used interchangeably for the aqueous ion of hydrogen. Hydronium ion and H3O+(aq) are preferred, but H+(aq) is also accepted on the AP Exam.
Water autoionizes with an equilibrium constant Kw. EQN: Kw = [H3O+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C
In pure water, pH = pOH is called a neutral solution. At 25°C, pKw = 14.0 and thus pH = pOH = 7.0. EQN: pKw = 14 = pH + pOH at 25°C
The value of Kw is temperature dependent, so the pH of pure, neutral water will deviate from 7.0 at temperatures other than 25°C.

depth_profile: Students must understand the autoionization of water and the relationship between hydronium and hydroxide ion concentrations. The course prefers the use of 'hydronium ion' and H3O+(aq), but 'hydrogen ion' and H+(aq) are also accepted. A key conceptual point is that Kw is temperature-dependent; therefore, the pH of pure, neutral water will deviate from 7.0 at temperatures other than 25°C.
assessment_notes: Questions may require calculating pH and pOH using Kw. Examiners will accept H+(aq) interchangeably with H3O+(aq) on the AP Exam. A common pitfall is assuming neutral water always has a pH of 7.0; students must recognize that neutral pH deviates from 7.0 at temperatures other than 25°C due to the temperature dependence of Kw.
reference_data:
Kw = [H3O+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C
pKw = 14 = pH + pOH at 25°C

spec_checklist.can_do: I can calculate the values of pH and pOH, based on Kw and the concentration of all species present in a neutral solution of water.
I can calculate pH and pOH from hydronium and hydroxide ion concentrations using negative logarithms.
I can use the autoionization constant of water (Kw = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C) to relate hydronium and hydroxide concentrations.
I can explain that a neutral solution is defined by pH = pOH, which is 7.0 at 25°C.
I can explain that because Kw is temperature dependent, the pH of pure, neutral water will deviate from 7.0 at temperatures other than 25°C.
